title: Allocate Minimum Pages
description: Allocate books minimizing maximum pages assigned

examples.explanation: We need to partition 4 books among 2 students. All possible partitions: Student1 gets books[0], Student2 gets books[1-3] → sums: 10 and 90 → max=90. Student1 gets books[0-1], Student2 gets books[2-3] → sums: 30 and 70 → max=70. Student1 gets books[0-2], Student2 gets books[3] → sums: 60 and 40 → max=60. Minimum max is 60.

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursive O(k * n^k)
approaches.summary: Try all possible partitions of books among students using recursion, calculating the maximum pages per student for each partition and tracking the minimum. This is a naive method that explores every combination but is impractical for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Recursive Allocation of Minimum Pages
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution trying all possible book partitions among students to minimize maximum pages per student.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force recursive solution to the 'Allocate Minimum Pages' problem. The intuition is to explore every possible way to partition n books among k students, calculating the maximum pages any student gets for each partition, and tracking the minimum of these maximums. This represents the optimal allocation where the most heavily loaded student has the smallest possible page count. The algorithm works by recursively assigning books to students: at each step, we decide how many books the current student gets (from 1 to remaining books minus remaining students plus 1), then recursively assign the remaining books to remaining students. For each complete partition, we calculate the maximum pages any student receives by summing pages for each student's assigned books, then track the minimum of these maximums. The time complexity is O(k * n^k) because we explore all combinations of partitions (n choose k-1 possibilities, roughly n^k) and for each we calculate sums for k students. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion depth. This approach is impractical for large inputs but demonstrates the fundamental exhaustive search strategy. Common mistakes include not handling the base case correctly (when only 1 student remains, they get all remaining books), incorrect bounds for how many books a student can get, and inefficient calculation of page sums. The connection to L1 is that this level implements the complete recursive framework that calls the partition exploration subroutine.

examples.trace: We need to partition 4 books among 2 students. All possible partitions: Student1 gets books[0], Student2 gets books[1-3] → sums: 10 and 90 → max=90. Student1 gets books[0-1], Student2 gets books[2-3] → sums: 30 and 70 → max=70. Student1 gets books[0-2], Student2 gets books[3] → sums: 60 and 40 → max=60. Minimum max is 60.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Partition Exploration
ladders.desc: Recursive function that generates all possible book partitions among students.
ladders.explanation: This is the core recursive subroutine that explores all possible ways to partition books among students. The intuition is to use recursion to systematically try every allocation: at each recursive call, we decide how many books the current student gets, then recursively assign the remaining books to the remaining students. This creates a tree of possibilities where each path represents one complete partition. The algorithm takes parameters: current book index, remaining students, current allocation array (tracking which books each student gets), and the global books array. At each step, we iterate through possible numbers of books for the current student (from 1 to remaining books minus remaining students plus 1, ensuring each subsequent student gets at least 1 book). We update the allocation array, then make a recursive call for the next student. When only 1 student remains (base case), they get all remaining books. The time complexity for this subroutine alone is O(n^k) as it generates all combinations. Space complexity is O(n) for recursion stack and allocation array. This feeds into L0 by providing the partition generation mechanism; L0 adds the page sum calculation and min-tracking on top of this. Common mistakes include incorrect bounds for the loop (not ensuring minimum 1 book per remaining student), forgetting to backtrack in the allocation array, and inefficient implementation that creates new arrays at each call. The connection to L2 is that this level implements the recursive structure that relies on the combinatorial partitioning insight.

examples.trace: First student can get 1 or 2 books (3 books total, 2 students → max 2 books for first). Option1: Take 1 book → allocation: student1=[A]. Recurse with books [B,C], 1 student left → student2 gets [B,C]. Option2: Take 2 books → allocation: student1=[A,B]. Recurse with books [C], 1 student left → student2 gets [C].

First student can get 1 or 2 books (4 books, 3 students → max 2). Option1: Take 1 book → student1=[X]. Recurse with [Y,Z,W], 2 students left. Second student can get 1 or 2 books from remaining 3. Sub-option1: Take 1 → student2=[Y], recurse with [Z,W], 1 student left → student3=[Z,W]. Sub-option2: Take 2 → student2=[Y,Z], recurse with [W], 1 student left → student3=[W]. Option2: First student takes 2 books → student1=[X,Y]. Recurse with [Z,W], 2 students left. Second student must take 1 book (only 2 books left, need 1 for third) → student2=[Z], recurse with [W], 1 student left → student3=[W].

testCases.input: books = [A, B, C, D, E], students = 2
books = [P, Q, R, S, T], students = 3

ladders.title: Core Logic — Combinatorial Partitioning of Sequence
ladders.desc: Understanding how to generate all ways to split a sequence into k contiguous groups.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core combinatorial insight: generating all possible ways to partition a sequence of n items into k contiguous groups. This is fundamental to the brute force approach. The intuition is that we need to place k-1 dividers between the n items, but since groups must be contiguous and non-empty, this becomes a stars-and-bars combinatorial problem. Specifically, we need to choose k-1 split points from the n-1 gaps between items. For example, with n=4 books and k=2 students, we have 3 gaps between books: after book1, after book2, after book3. Choosing 1 split point gives us 3 possible partitions. The algorithm to generate these involves recursive decision-making: at each step, decide how many items go into the current group (at least 1), then recursively partition the remaining items among remaining groups. The number of possible partitions is C(n-1, k-1) where C is combination. Time complexity to generate all is exponential in k. This feeds into L1 by providing the mathematical foundation for the recursive exploration. Common mistakes include generating non-contiguous partitions (not allowed since books must be in order), allowing empty groups, or incorrect counting of possibilities. The connection to L3 is that this combinatorial understanding relies on basic recursive decision-making patterns.

examples.trace: Items: [A,B,C,D]. Gaps: after A, after B, after C. Choose 1 split point: Split after A → groups: [A] and [B,C,D]. Split after B → groups: [A,B] and [C,D]. Split after C → groups: [A,B,C] and [D].

Items: [A,B,C,D,E]. Need 2 split points from 4 gaps. Combinations: gaps (1,2), (1,3), (1,4), (2,3), (2,4), (3,4). Example: splits after A and C → groups: [A], [B,C], [D,E] → sizes (1,2,2). Splits after B and D → groups: [A,B], [C,D], [E] → sizes (2,2,1).

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Decision Making with Bounds
ladders.desc: Implementing recursion with constraints on choices at each step.
ladders.explanation: This level teaches how to implement recursive functions where at each step you make a decision with constrained options. This is a fundamental pattern in combinatorial algorithms. The intuition is that recursion breaks a problem into smaller subproblems, and at each recursive call we choose from a limited set of possibilities based on remaining resources. For the book allocation problem, the constraints are: each student must get at least 1 book, and books must be assigned in order. So when deciding how many books to give the current student, we can take between 1 and (remaining books - remaining students + 1) books. This ensures enough books left for other students. The algorithm structure: base case when only 1 student remains (they get all remaining books), recursive case where we loop through possible allocations for current student, make recursive call for next student with updated parameters. Time complexity depends on branching factor; space is O(recursion depth). This feeds into L2 by providing the implementation mechanism for combinatorial partitioning. Common mistakes include incorrect base case handling, off-by-one errors in bounds calculation, and not maintaining state properly across recursive calls. The connection to L4 is that this recursive pattern relies on basic iteration and condition checking operations.

examples.input: Remaining books=5, remaining students=3, current book index=0
examples.output: Current student can take 1, 2, or 3 books
examples.trace: Calculation: max books = remaining books - remaining students + 1 = 5 - 3 + 1 = 3. Minimum is 1. So options: take 1 book (then 4 books left for 2 students), take 2 books (3 books left for 2 students), take 3 books (2 books left for 2 students). Each option leads to recursive call.

examples.input: Remaining books=3, remaining students=2, current book index=2
examples.output: Current student can take 1 or 2 books
examples.trace: Max books = 3 - 2 + 1 = 2. Options: take 1 book (2 books left for 1 student), take 2 books (1 book left for 1 student). Base case will handle when 1 student remains.

testCases.expected: Each gets 1 book: take 1, then recurse with (4,4), then 1, (3,3), 1, (2,2), 1, (1,1)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating with Constraint Checking
ladders.desc: Looping through possible values while satisfying conditions.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental programming operation of iterating through a range of values while checking constraints. This is a basic building block used in the recursive decision-making. The intuition is simple: when we need to try multiple possibilities, we use a loop that goes from a minimum to a maximum value, and at each iteration we ensure the current choice is valid given the problem constraints. For book allocation, the constraint is that we must leave enough books for the remaining students (at least 1 book per student). So the loop goes from 1 to (remaining books - remaining students + 1). The algorithm: calculate min=1, max=remaining - students + 1, then for i in range(min, max+1): try allocating i books. This ensures we never allocate so many books that we can't give at least 1 to each remaining student. Time complexity is O(max-min) for the loop. This feeds into L3 by providing the iteration mechanism within each recursive call. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in range calculation, not handling edge cases (like when remaining books equals remaining students), and inefficient repeated calculation of bounds. The connection to L5 is that this operation relies on basic arithmetic and comparison operations.

examples.trace: Calculate max = 7 - 3 + 1 = 5. Min = 1. So i values: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Check: If i=5, then 7-5=2 books left for 2 students → OK (1 each). If i=6 would leave 1 book for 2 students → invalid.
Max = 4 - 4 + 1 = 1. Min = 1. So only i=1. This makes sense: with 4 books for 4 students, first student must take exactly 1 book.

testCases.expected: Loop 1 to 8 (8-1+1=8) but actually base case handles

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Basic Recursion with Parameters
ladders.desc: Understanding simple recursive functions that pass state through parameters.
ladders.explanation: This is the most fundamental level: understanding basic recursion where a function calls itself with modified parameters. This is prerequisite for all recursive algorithms. The intuition is that recursion solves problems by breaking them into smaller, similar subproblems. A recursive function has two parts: base case(s) that handle the simplest version of the problem directly, and recursive case(s) that call the function with different parameters. For example, a function that calculates factorial: factorial(n) returns 1 if n=0 (base case), else returns n * factorial(n-1) (recursive case). Parameters carry the state (like current value of n). Time complexity depends on number of recursive calls; space complexity includes recursion stack. This feeds into L4 by providing the foundational pattern that enables constrained iteration within recursive calls. Common mistakes include missing base case (causing infinite recursion), not properly updating parameters, and misunderstanding how recursion uses stack memory. The connection to the full problem is that this basic pattern is extended with more complex parameters (book index, remaining students) and constraints.

examples.trace: factorial(4) calls factorial(3) → factorial(3) calls factorial(2) → factorial(2) calls factorial(1) → factorial(1) calls factorial(0) → factorial(0) returns 1 (base case). Then unwind: factorial(1) returns 1*1=1, factorial(2) returns 2*1=2, factorial(3) returns 3*2=6, factorial(4) returns 4*6=24.

sum_first_n(3) returns 3 + sum_first_n(2). sum_first_n(2) returns 2 + sum_first_n(1). sum_first_n(1) returns 1 + sum_first_n(0). sum_first_n(0) returns 0 (base case). Unwind: sum_first_n(1)=1+0=1, sum_first_n(2)=2+1=3, sum_first_n(3)=3+3=6.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming O(n^2 * k)
approaches.summary: Use DP where dp[i][j] represents the minimum maximum pages for i books and j students, building solutions from subproblems. This avoids recomputation but still has high polynomial complexity.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Allocate Minimum Pages with DP O(n²·k)
ladders.desc: Complete solution using DP where dp[i][j] = min max pages for i books and j students.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires distributing n books with given page counts among k students such that each student gets at least one book, books are allocated in contiguous order, and the maximum pages assigned to any student is minimized. The DP approach builds solutions from subproblems: dp[i][j] represents the minimum possible maximum pages when allocating the first i books among j students. The recurrence is: dp[i][j] = min_{p=1 to i} max(dp[p-1][j-1], sum(pages[p..i])). Base cases: dp[i][1] = sum of first i books (one student gets all), and dp[0][j] = 0 (no books). The intuition is to try all possible splits where the last student gets books from p to i, and take the minimum of the worst-case maximum. Time: O(n²·k) from nested loops over i, j, and p. Space: O(n·k) for DP table. Connection: This integrates all lower-level concepts into the final algorithm. Common mistakes: Forgetting base cases, incorrect indexing in sum calculation, or misunderstanding the min-max logic. Real code pattern: Initialize DP table, compute prefix sums for O(1) range sums, fill base cases, then iterate i from 1 to n, j from 2 to k, p from 1 to i, update dp[i][j] = min(max(dp[p-1][j-1], prefix[i]-prefix[p-1])). Return dp[n][k].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Recurrence Evaluation
ladders.desc: Compute dp[i][j] using the recurrence: min over splits of max(dp[p-1][j-1], sum(pages[p..i])).
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the DP solution. Given i books and j students, we need to find the optimal split point p (1 ≤ p ≤ i) where the last student gets books from p to i. The maximum pages for this allocation is the maximum between: (1) the minimum maximum pages for the first p-1 books allocated to j-1 students (dp[p-1][j-1]), and (2) the sum of pages from p to i. We take the minimum over all possible p. Intuition: By trying all splits, we ensure we consider every way to assign the last student's books, and choose the one that minimizes the worst-case load. Step-by-step: For fixed i and j, iterate p from 1 to i, compute candidate = max(dp[p-1][j-1], prefix_sum[i] - prefix_sum[p-1]), and keep track of the minimum candidate. Time: O(i) per dp[i][j] evaluation. Space: O(1) extra. Connection: This is used in the full problem's inner loop. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in indices, or not handling p=1 correctly where dp[0][j-1] is a base case. Real code pattern: min_val = INF; for p in range(1, i+1): candidate = max(dp[p-1][j-1], prefix[i] - prefix[p-1]); min_val = min(min_val, candidate); dp[i][j] = min_val.

examples.input: i=3, j=2, dp table with base filled, prefix=[0,10,30,60], pages=[10,20,30]

testCases.input: i=0, j=2, dp: dp[0][1]=0, prefix=[0], pages=[]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Minimax Optimization via Splitting
ladders.desc: The insight of minimizing the maximum by trying all split points and taking the minimum of the worst-case.
ladders.explanation: This core logic captures the essential optimization strategy: to minimize the maximum pages assigned to any student, we need to consider all possible ways to split the book sequence among students, and for each split, the maximum pages is determined by the heavier of the two parts (previous students' allocation vs. current student's books). By taking the minimum over all splits, we find the optimal balance. Intuition: Imagine distributing workload; if you give too many books to the last student, their load is high; if too few, the previous students might be overloaded. The minimax approach ensures the peak load is as low as possible. Step-by-step: For a given set of books and number of students, recursively define the optimal value as: f(books, k) = min_{split} max(f(books_before_split, k-1), sum(books_after_split)). This is a divide-and-conquer idea implemented via DP. Time: Depends on implementation, but conceptually exponential without DP. Space: O(1) for the logic itself. Connection: This logic is implemented in the DP recurrence. Common mistakes: Confusing min and max, or not considering all splits. Real code pattern: Represented in the recurrence relation; no standalone code but the idea drives the algorithm.

examples.trace: We need to split into 2 groups. Possible splits: Split after book 1: group1=[10], group2=[20,30]. max(10, 50)=50. Split after book 2: group1=[10,20], group2=[30]. max(30, 30)=30. Minimum over splits: min(50,30)=30. So optimal max pages is 30.

ladders.title: Building Block — Prefix Sums for Range Queries
ladders.desc: Compute cumulative sums to get sum of any subarray in O(1) time.
ladders.explanation: Prefix sums are an array where prefix[i] stores the sum of the first i elements of the original array. For an array pages of length n, define prefix[0] = 0, and for i from 1 to n, prefix[i] = prefix[i-1] + pages[i-1]. Then, the sum of pages from index l to r (1-based, inclusive) is prefix[r] - prefix[l-1]. Intuition: By precomputing cumulative sums, we avoid repeatedly summing the same ranges, which would take O(n) time per query. This is crucial for the DP recurrence where we need many range sums. Step-by-step: Initialize prefix array of size n+1. Set prefix[0]=0. Loop i from 1 to n: prefix[i] = prefix[i-1] + pages[i-1]. To query sum(l, r): return prefix[r] - prefix[l-1]. Time: O(n) to build, O(1) per query. Space: O(n). Connection: Used in the DP recurrence to compute sum(pages[p..i]) quickly. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors in indices, or using 0-based indexing inconsistently. Real code pattern: prefix = [0]*(n+1); for i in range(1, n+1): prefix[i] = prefix[i-1] + pages[i-1]; sum_l_r = prefix[r] - prefix[l-1].

examples.trace: Initialize prefix[0]=0. i=1: prefix[1] = prefix[0] + pages[0] = 0+10=10. i=2: prefix[2] = prefix[1] + pages[1] = 10+20=30. i=3: prefix[3] = prefix[2] + pages[2] = 30+30=60. i=4: prefix[4] = prefix[3] + pages[3] = 60+40=100. So prefix array is [0,10,30,60,100].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Summation
ladders.desc: Compute the sum of elements in a subarray.
ladders.explanation: This operation involves adding up all numbers in a contiguous segment of an array. Given an array pages and indices l and r (0-based or 1-based), the sum is pages[l] + pages[l+1] + ... + pages[r]. Intuition: Summation is fundamental in many algorithms; here, it represents the total pages a student would read if assigned books from l to r. Step-by-step: Initialize total = 0. Loop from index = l to r (inclusive): total += pages[index]. Return total. Time: O(r-l+1) linear in the size of the subarray. Space: O(1). Connection: Used to compute range sums before optimization with prefix sums. Common mistakes: Incorrect loop bounds, or using wrong indexing. Real code pattern: sum = 0; for i in range(l, r+1): sum += pages[i]; return sum.

examples.trace: Convert to 0-based: l=0, r=2. Sum = pages[0] + pages[1] + pages[2] = 10 + 20 + 30 = 60. Wait, 1-based l=1 means first element 10, r=3 means up to 40? Clarify: In 1-based, pages[1]=10, pages[2]=20, pages[3]=30, pages[4]=40. l=1 to r=3: sum = 10+20+30 = 60. But if l=1, r=4: sum=10+20+30+40=100. Let's assume l=1, r=3 gives 60. Step: indices 1,2,3: values 10,20,30; sum=60.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Understand arrays, zero-based vs one-based indexing, and accessing elements.
ladders.explanation: An array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements, each identified by an index. In programming, arrays are typically zero-based: the first element is at index 0, second at index 1, etc. For an array arr of length n, valid indices are 0 to n-1. One-based indexing is sometimes used in problems for clarity: index 1 refers to the first element. It's crucial to be consistent. Intuition: Arrays allow random access to elements in O(1) time, making them efficient for many algorithms. Step-by-step: Declare an array, e.g., pages = [10,20,30]. Access: pages[0] gives 10, pages[1] gives 20. Length: len(pages) = 3. Time: O(1) for access. Space: O(n) to store n elements. Connection: All higher levels use arrays to represent book pages. Common mistakes: Index out of bounds (e.g., accessing index 3 in a 3-element array with zero-based indexing), or confusing zero-based and one-based. Real code pattern: In Python, lists are used as arrays; pages = [10,20,30]; first = pages[0]; last = pages[-1] or pages[len(pages)-1].

examples.trace: Zero-based indexing: index 0: 12, index 1: 34, index 2: 67, index 3: 90. So pages[2] = 67.

One-based indexing: index 1: 10, index 2: 20, index 3: 30, index 4: 40. So pages[3] = 30? Wait, if we treat the array as one-based, we need to adjust: typically in code, arrays are zero-based, so to simulate one-based, we access pages[index-1]. So for index 3, access pages[2] = 30. But if the problem states one-based, we might store data starting at index 1. Clarify: In explanations, we'll use zero-based for code, but in problem descriptions, indices may be one-based. For this example, if array is stored as [10,20,30,40] with zero-based, and we want one-based index 3, we access pages[2] = 30. But if we want the third element in one-based sense, it's 30. So output 30.
